summaries: Between 10:15 and 15:42 UTC on 08 Apr 2026, customers using {service} on Amazon EKS in the us-east-1 Region experienced elevated request latency. CPU utilization on pod {pod} reached the cgroup limit of {cpu_limit}, and the Linux CFS scheduler throttled {throttle_pct}% of scheduling periods. P99 latency increased from {slo} ms to {latency} ms. No pod restarts or memory pressure were observed. The deployment's CPU limit was adjusted at 15:42 UTC.

Tracking ID: AZ-CP8-20260410 — Between approximately 08:30 and 13:00 UTC on 10 Apr 2026, a subset of customers using Azure Kubernetes Service in the East US region observed progressive latency degradation for {service}. Container CPU utilization was capped at the configured limit of {cpu_limit}. The CFS throttle rate reached {throttle_pct}%, driving p99 response times to {latency} ms. No OOM events were recorded. The CPU limit was increased at 13:00 UTC to mitigate the issue.

03:45 PM PDT — We are investigating elevated latency for {service} in the us-west1 region. CPU throttle events have been occurring since approximately 02:00 PM PDT, with {throttle_count} throttle events logged in the last hour. Container CPU usage is pegged at {cpu_pct}% of the {cpu_limit} cgroup quota. P50 latency is {p50} ms and p99 is {p99} ms. The service remains up and processing requests at reduced throughput.

Google Cloud Status — The issue with Google Kubernetes Engine affecting {service} in europe-west1 has been resolved as of 11:30 US/Pacific. Between 06:00 and 11:30 US/Pacific, the container CFS scheduler throttled {service} pods at a rate of {throttle_pct}% due to CPU limit exhaustion at {cpu_limit}. Latency degraded progressively from baseline to p99={latency} ms. No crashes or data loss occurred. The CPU quota was increased as a mitigation.

Tracking ID: 8_P5-RK1 — Summary of impact: Between approximately 17:00 and 22:30 UTC on 11 Apr 2026, customers accessing {service} on Azure Container Apps in West Europe experienced sustained latency increases. CPU throttle count reached {throttle_count} events over the incident window. P99 latency climbed from baseline to {latency} ms while p50 remained at {p50} ms. Memory metrics were nominal throughout. CPU limits were adjusted at 22:30 UTC.

04/07/2026 EKS CPU Throttling Incident — Resolved — Between 09:20 and 14:55 PDT, {service} pods in the ap-southeast-1 Region experienced progressive request slowdown due to CPU quota exhaustion. The kernel CFS scheduler throttled pod {pod} at {throttle_pct}% of scheduling periods, with {throttle_count} total throttle events logged. P99 latency reached {latency} ms. The service continued operating without crashes or OOM events throughout the incident.

Between 21:00 and 02:30 UTC on 06–07 Apr 2026, a subset of customers running {service} in Google Cloud region us-central1 experienced increased response times. Container CPU utilization reached the configured limit of {cpu_limit} and remained there for the duration of the incident. CFS throttling drove p99 latency to {latency} ms and p50 to {p50} ms. Pod health checks passed throughout. The issue was resolved by increasing the CPU limit.

We experienced degraded request latency for {service} in the eu-west-1 Region between 11:30 and 16:45 UTC. Container CPU was consistently throttled at {throttle_pct}% by the cgroup CFS quota mechanism. Throttle event count reached {throttle_count} over the incident window. P99 latency was {latency} ms vs an SLO of {slo} ms. No memory anomalies were detected. CPU limit adjustment at 16:45 UTC restored normal latency.

Tracking ID: AZ-KS3-20260412 — Customers using Azure Kubernetes Service in the Japan East region reported latency increases for {service} between 05:00 and 09:45 UTC on 12 Apr 2026. CPU utilization for the affected container reached {cpu_pct}% of the {cpu_limit} limit. The CFS scheduler issued {throttle_count} throttle events during the incident. P99 latency reached {latency} ms. No restarts were recorded. CPU limits were increased to resolve the issue.

07:55 PM PDT — Amazon ECS tasks running {service} in the ca-central-1 Region are experiencing elevated latency due to CPU throttling. Container CPU is at {cpu_pct}% of the {cpu_limit} task limit. The CFS scheduler has issued {throttle_count} throttle events in the current hour. P99 latency is {latency} ms against an SLO of {slo} ms. The service is operational but degraded. We are adjusting CPU limits.

Google Cloud Status Update — 14:00 US/Pacific — We are investigating latency degradation for {service} in the asia-northeast1 region. CPU throttle events began accumulating at 10:30 US/Pacific. The container is consuming its full CPU quota of {cpu_limit} each scheduling period, resulting in {throttle_pct}% of periods being throttled. P50 latency is {p50} ms, p99 is {p99} ms. Memory and pod health metrics are normal.

Between 16:00 and 20:30 UTC on 13 Apr 2026, customers running {service} on Amazon EKS in the eu-central-1 Region experienced progressive latency degradation. CPU was pinned at the {cpu_limit} cgroup limit, causing the CFS scheduler to throttle {throttle_pct}% of periods. Throttle events totaled {throttle_count}. P99 latency reached {latency} ms. The service did not crash or experience memory pressure at any point during the incident.

Tracking ID: GCP-INC-20260409-002 — Between 03:15 and 08:00 UTC on 09 Apr 2026, GKE clusters in europe-west2 running {service} experienced sustained latency degradation. The container CPU utilization was capped at {cpu_limit} throughout the incident. CFS throttle rate reached {throttle_pct}%, with {throttle_count} throttle events recorded. P99 latency was {latency} ms vs a {slo} ms SLO. No container restarts occurred.

04/15/2026 CPU Throttling Incident — Mitigated — Tracking ID: AZ-WE2-20260415 — Between 12:00 and 17:45 UTC, {service} on Azure Kubernetes Service in the Australia Southeast region experienced request slowdown due to CFS CPU throttling. Container CPU usage reached {cpu_pct}% of the {cpu_limit} limit. Throttle events accumulated at a rate corresponding to {throttle_pct}% of scheduling periods. P99 latency peaked at {latency} ms. Mitigation was applied at 17:45 UTC by increasing the CPU limit.

10:18 AM PDT — We have resolved the latency issue affecting {service} pods in the us-east-2 Region. Between 07:30 and 10:18 AM PDT, container CPU was throttled by the CFS scheduler at {throttle_pct}% of periods. Throttle event count reached {throttle_count}. P99 latency peaked at {latency} ms vs a {slo} ms SLO. No OOM events or pod restarts were observed at any point. CPU limit was increased to restore normal response times.

Between 19:00 and 23:30 UTC on 14 Apr 2026, customers in the Google Cloud europe-west4 region running {service} experienced elevated p99 response times. The container CPU quota of {cpu_limit} was exhausted each scheduling period, causing the kernel to issue {throttle_count} throttle events. P99 latency degraded from baseline to {latency} ms. P50 was less affected at {p50} ms. Pod health checks returned healthy throughout the incident.

Tracking ID: AZ-NC2-20260416 — Summary of impact: Between approximately 09:00 and 14:15 UTC on 16 Apr 2026, a subset of Azure Kubernetes Service customers in the Korea Central region running {service} experienced elevated latency. The container CPU was throttled at {throttle_pct}% of CFS scheduling periods, accumulating {throttle_count} throttle events. P99 latency reached {p99} ms. No memory pressure, OOM events, or pod restarts were observed.

11:05 PM PDT — Amazon EKS pods running {service} in the ap-northeast-2 Region are experiencing CPU throttle-driven latency degradation. Google Cloud Status — Resolved — 10:45 US/Pacific — The latency incident affecting {service} in GKE clusters in northamerica-northeast1 has been resolved. Between 05:15 and 10:45 US/Pacific, the container CFS scheduler throttled {throttle_pct}% of scheduling periods due to CPU quota exhaustion at {cpu_limit}. P99 latency peaked at {latency} ms. No pod restarts or memory anomalies occurred. CPU limits were increased as the mitigation.

Between 08:00 and 12:30 UTC on 05 Apr 2026, customers running {service} on Amazon EKS in the me-south-1 Region experienced degraded response times without any pod restarts or availability impact.
